items.q: When can a laissez-faire leadership style be effective?
items.correct: When employees are highly skilled, self-motivated and need autonomy — common in creative or expert teams. It can fail with inexperienced staff or in crises requiring direction.
items.misconception: Laissez-faire leadership means the leader doesn't care about the business.
items.random_wrong: Laissez-faire leadership is illegal under employment law.
items.why: Laissez-faire is a deliberate choice to delegate, not negligence. Students moralise it as lazy management, missing its appropriateness for skilled autonomous workers and innovation-led settings.

items.q: When might an autocratic leadership style be appropriate?
items.correct: In crises requiring quick decisions, when workers are inexperienced, or in highly hierarchical contexts — though it can damage morale long-term.
items.misconception: Autocratic leadership is always wrong and democratic leadership is always right.
items.random_wrong: Autocratic leaders are dictators who break the law.
items.why: Students moralise leadership styles. The business studies view is contingency-based — each style has appropriate contexts. Democratic is not universally best; autocracy works in emergencies.

items.q: What are Porter's two main generic strategies for competitive advantage?
items.correct: Cost leadership (being the lowest-cost producer) and differentiation (offering unique value customers will pay more for) — Porter argues firms should choose one or the other.
items.misconception: Porter says businesses should pursue both low cost and differentiation at once.
items.random_wrong: Porter's strategies are about marketing channels.
items.why: Porter's central warning is against being 'stuck in the middle'. Students assume more strategies = better; Porter argues choosing one focus is more effective than trying both.

items.q: Modigliani and Miller's irrelevance theorem was published in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1958. “1948” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Cost of capital. “Annual cost calculation” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Modigliani and Miller publish their capital structure theorem?
items.why: Exact answer: 1958 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
